title: Do Blue Light Glasses Work? What the Research Actually Says
description: Do blue light glasses reduce eye strain and improve sleep? Here's what the science says, what they can and can't do, and whether they're worth buying.
tldr: Blue light glasses filter a portion of blue-violet light from screens and artificial lighting. Research shows they don't significantly reduce digital eye strain (which is caused by focusing, not light wavelength), but they may offer a small benefit for sleep if worn in the evening.

tldrStats.label: Year of major Cochrane review on blue light glasses
Proven benefit for digital eye strain (per research)
Of blue-violet light filtered by most blue light lenses

definition.text: Blue light glasses (also called blue light blocking glasses or blue cut lenses) are eyeglasses with lenses that filter or absorb a portion of blue-violet light (approximately 415-455nm wavelength) emitted by digital screens, LED lighting, and sunlight. They are marketed primarily for reducing digital eye strain and improving sleep quality.

sections.content:
Blue light is part of the visible light spectrum, with wavelengths between roughly 380-500 nanometres. It's everywhere — sunlight is the biggest source by far. Screens, LED bulbs, and fluorescent lighting also emit blue light, but at a fraction of the intensity you'd get from spending five minutes outside on a cloudy day.

There are two subcategories worth knowing: blue-violet light (380-455nm), which is the higher-energy portion that some researchers have studied for potential retinal effects, and blue-turquoise light (455-500nm), which is thought to play a role in regulating your sleep-wake cycle.

Understanding the terminology helps you make informed decisions when shopping for glasses and communicating with your eye care provider. Many people feel overwhelmed by the technical language used in optical stores — but the concepts are actually straightforward once you know what each term means. Don't hesitate to ask your optician to explain anything you're unsure about. A good optician takes the time to educate rather than just sell.

At Charm Optical in Edmonton, the staff walks every patient through their options in plain language — no jargon, no pressure. Whether you're buying your first pair of glasses or upgrading to progressive lenses, they explain what each choice means for your vision and your budget. Book an eye exam to start with an up-to-date prescription, then take your time choosing frames and lenses with expert guidance.

sections.h2: The Claim: Blue Light Glasses Reduce Eye Strain
sections.content:
This is the big one. Most people buy blue light glasses because their eyes feel tired, dry, or strained after long hours on a screen. The logic seems straightforward: screens emit blue light, blue light glasses block blue light, therefore less eye strain. Right?

Not quite. A major 2023 Cochrane systematic review — the gold standard for medical evidence — analysed 17 randomized controlled trials and found no meaningful evidence that blue light filtering lenses reduce digital eye strain compared to non-filtering lenses.

The reason: digital eye strain (sometimes called computer vision syndrome) isn't caused by blue light. It's caused by:

  • Reduced blinking: You blink about 66% less when staring at a screen, leading to dry eyes
  • Sustained focusing: Holding focus at a fixed distance for hours fatigues the ciliary muscles in your eyes
  • Poor ergonomics: Screen too close, too far, too bright, too dim, or at the wrong angle

These are mechanical and behavioural issues — a tinted lens doesn't address any of them.

sections.h2: The Claim: Blue Light Glasses Improve Sleep
sections.content:
This one has slightly more nuance. Blue-turquoise light does play a role in suppressing melatonin production. Exposure to bright blue-enriched light in the evening can delay your circadian rhythm, making it harder to fall asleep.

Some small studies have shown that wearing blue light blocking lenses in the 2-3 hours before bed may provide a modest improvement in sleep onset for some people. But the Cochrane review noted that the overall evidence quality was low, and the effect sizes were small.

More effective strategies for screen-related sleep issues include: using your device's built-in night mode (which shifts the entire screen to warmer tones), dimming your screen brightness, and simply putting your phone down 30-60 minutes before bed.

This is one of the most common questions that comes up during eye exams, and the answer often depends on individual factors that only a professional assessment can evaluate. What works for one person may not be ideal for another — your prescription, daily activities, facial anatomy, and budget all play a role in determining the best approach.

sections.h2: Do Blue Light Glasses Protect Your Eyes from Damage?
sections.content:
Some marketing suggests that blue light from screens causes retinal damage. The evidence doesn't support this concern for normal screen use. The blue light emitted by a typical monitor or phone is orders of magnitude less intense than what you'd get from natural sunlight.

The Canadian Association of Optometrists has noted that there is currently no scientific evidence that blue light from digital devices causes damage to the eye. The American Academy of Ophthalmology has taken a similar position.

That said, long-term research on cumulative low-level blue light exposure is still limited. The topic isn't fully settled — it's just that the current evidence doesn't indicate a problem.

This is one of the most common questions that comes up during eye exams, and the answer often depends on individual factors that only a professional assessment can evaluate. sections.h2: What Actually Helps with Screen-Related Eye Strain
sections.content:
If your eyes feel tired after screen time, these strategies have much stronger evidence behind them than blue light lenses:

  • The 20-20-20 rule: Every 20 minutes, look at something 20 feet away for 20 seconds. This relaxes the focusing muscles in your eyes.
  • Blink more deliberately: Make a conscious effort to blink fully while using screens. Some people benefit from artificial tears if dryness persists.
  • Optimize your screen setup: Position your monitor 20-26 inches from your eyes, slightly below eye level. Adjust brightness to match your environment.
  • Get the right prescription: An outdated or incorrect prescription forces your eyes to work harder. If you haven't had an eye exam in the past year, that's a good place to start.
  • Consider computer glasses: Single vision lenses tuned to your monitor distance, or occupational progressives with a wider intermediate zone, can make a real difference for people who spend 6+ hours at a screen daily.

sections.h2: So Should You Buy Blue Light Glasses?
sections.content:
Here's the honest answer: if you like the slight yellow tint and feel subjectively more comfortable wearing them, go for it. They won't hurt your eyes and they're not expensive to add as a coating. Just know that the scientific evidence for their benefits is weak at best.

What you should absolutely do before (or instead of) buying blue light glasses:

  • Get an up-to-date eye exam — many cases of "screen strain" are actually uncorrected or under-corrected vision problems
  • Ask your optometrist about computer glasses or occupational lenses if you work at a screen all day
  • Practice the 20-20-20 rule consistently
  • Check whether your insurance plan covers blue light coating if you do decide to add it — many Alberta benefit plans include lens coatings

If you want to discuss whether blue light lenses make sense for your specific situation, the opticians at Charm Optical in South Edmonton can give you a straight answer based on your prescription and daily habits.

sections.h2: How to Choose the Right Lenses
sections.content:
Choosing the right lenses starts with understanding your prescription and daily visual demands. Your optometrist will assess your refraction, measure your pupillary distance, and discuss your lifestyle needs — whether you spend most of your day on screens, driving, reading, or working outdoors. Each of these activities benefits from specific lens designs and coatings.

Lens material matters too. Standard CR-39 plastic is affordable and optically clear, but higher prescriptions benefit from high-index materials (1.60, 1.67, or 1.74) that produce thinner, lighter lenses. Polycarbonate and Trivex offer impact resistance for active lifestyles and children's glasses. The right combination of material, design, and coatings gives you the clearest, most comfortable vision possible.

The best way to navigate these choices is with professional guidance. A comprehensive eye exam at Charm Optical includes a detailed discussion of lens options tailored to your specific needs. The opticians at Charm Optical in Edmonton can walk you through each option side by side, and many lens types are available for same-day glasses at Charm Optical, so you don't have to wait days for your new glasses.

Your eyes are one of the most important tools you have, and they deserve proper care and correction. The technology behind modern eyewear has advanced remarkably in recent years — from digital freeform progressive lenses that customize the optics to your exact wearing position, to lightweight titanium frames that weigh less than a coin, to anti-reflective coatings that virtually eliminate glare. Understanding these options empowers you to make better choices about your eye health and get glasses that genuinely improve your quality of life rather than just meeting a basic need.

Regular eye exams are the foundation of good vision care. The Canadian Association of Optometrists recommends exams every one to two years for adults and annually for children, seniors over 65, and anyone with diabetes or a family history of eye disease. A comprehensive exam does far more than measure your prescription — your optometrist checks the health of your retina, optic nerve, and blood vessels, catching conditions like glaucoma and macular degeneration in their earliest stages. Many serious eye conditions have no noticeable symptoms until significant damage has occurred, making preventive exams essential.

The best next step is always a professional eye exam. Your optometrist can evaluate your specific needs and recommend the right combination of lenses, coatings, and frames for your lifestyle and budget. In Alberta, most employer insurance plans cover comprehensive eye exams, and children under 18 and adults over 65 receive coverage through Alberta Health.

Charm Optical at 5035 Ellerslie Rd SW in South Edmonton offers thorough eye exams with experienced optometrists, a selection of over 500 designer frames from brands like Ray-Ban, Oakley, and Versace, and same-day glasses for many prescription types. faqs.q: Do optometrists recommend blue light glasses?
faqs.a: Most optometrists take a measured position. The Canadian Association of Optometrists acknowledges that there's no strong evidence blue light glasses reduce eye strain or prevent eye damage from screens. However, many optometrists will offer blue light coating as an option if a patient wants it, noting that it's harmless and some people report subjective comfort improvements.

faqs.q: Are blue light glasses the same as computer glasses?
faqs.a: No. Computer glasses are prescription lenses optimized for your specific screen distance and may include progressive or single vision designs. Blue light glasses simply filter certain wavelengths and don't address focusing distance. Computer glasses are far more effective for reducing digital eye strain. Ask about computer lenses at Charm Optical.

faqs.q: Can kids wear blue light glasses?
faqs.a: Blue light glasses won't harm children's eyes, but they're not a substitute for healthy screen habits. The Canadian Paediatric Society recommends limiting screen time and encouraging outdoor play — which is far more evidence-based than any lens coating for protecting children's eye health.

faqs.q: How much do blue light glasses cost in Canada?
faqs.a: If added as a coating to prescription lenses, blue light filtering typically costs $30-80 extra. Non-prescription blue light glasses are available for $15-50 at most retailers. The more effective investment is usually a proper pair of computer glasses with the right prescription.

faqs.q: What lens material is best for strong prescriptions?
faqs.a: High-index lenses (1.67 or 1.74) are best for strong prescriptions because they bend light more efficiently, resulting in thinner and lighter lenses. Standard plastic (CR-39) works well for mild prescriptions. Your optician at Charm Optical can recommend the ideal index based on your prescription strength.

faqs.q: How often should I replace my lenses?
faqs.a: Most optometrists recommend updating your prescription every 1-2 years. Lens coatings (anti-reflective, scratch-resistant) degrade over time even if your prescription hasn't changed. If you notice scratches, haze, or peeling, it's time for new lenses. Book an eye exam at Charm Optical to check your current prescription.
